scores.design: Curio Desktop-First Monolith — "one app, files are the API"
scores.notes: OSS strengths: lowest-friction install story (download one signed app), the post-Omnivore 'files you keep' trust pitch is the best README headline of the three, packager-friendly (no bundled yt-dlp, XDG-correct storage split, updater disabled in deb/rpm/Flatpak — Flathub/AUR/nixpkgs viable), and the 7-crate split means most contributor PRs run under plain cargo test. Demo lands: GIF of keystroke-promote → markdown appearing in an Obsidian vault is genuinely novel. ADVERSARIAL — weakest claim: 'the 2026 market seat is literally empty' is overstated (NewsFlash, Raven, Feedbin+Reeder ecosystems exist) and dodges the real adoption killer: the first question on every HN/lobste.rs thread will be 'does it sync?' and this design's honest answer ('no, use OPML + a tarball') caps the star ceiling hard. Second weak claim: file-journal union+LWW sync as 'an additive feature, no CRDT' is famously not near-free (clock skew, compaction, deleted-feed refs — its own risk list admits this). No CLI/scripting surface in v1 also cuts off the agent/automation audience the landscape says is emerging, and gives contributors only one way in (the GUI app).

OSS strengths: best hack-on-it story of the three — curio-core is contributable with zero webview toolchain (Tauri Linux dev-env pain is a real, documented contributor deterrent), and cargo-deny mechanically enforces the boundary rather than review policing it. The v1 CLI is the sleeper adoption asset: `cargo install curio-cli` / brew formula is trivially packageable, gives a second README demo (asciinema: add feed → fetch → export → events tail) alongside the desktop GIF, serves the cron/agent/quantified-self crowd no incumbent serves, and doubles as CI proof the boundary holds. crates.io publication of curio-core opens an adoption channel the other designs lack entirely (TUI readers, MCP servers, other projects embedding the hard 20%). Both futures (server head → homelab stars; GReader client mode → mobile answer) stay cheap without v1 scope. ADVERSARIAL — weakest claim: 'curio-core IS the product.' For stars it isn't — the screenshot is. Library-first framing risks a beautiful crate under a mediocre app, and 'other projects will embed it' is speculative demand (papr/MCP niche is thin evidence). The in-repo curio-server stub is a standing invitation for scope-creep issues its own governance must repeatedly decline. Sync answer at v1 is identical to design 1's weakness — same HN question, same soft answer, mitigated only by a visible roadmap seam.

OSS strengths: the homelab pattern is the proven star engine (Miniflux/FreshRSS-class projects, awesome-selfhosted listing, Docker+systemd+SBOM = packagers' favorite artifact), GReader/Fever compat gives the only real day-one mobile answer, and one-binary+SQLite deliberately fixes Miniflux's #1 adoption complaint. Real Playwright-in-browser E2E is the most honest test story. ADVERSARIAL — weakest claim: 'sync by construction, not bolted on' — the replay outbox with LWW, standalone→remote migration, and embedded-server lifecycle (WAL locks after force-kill, sleep/wake, AV on Windows loopback) are reconciliation-and-ops problems by other names; its own risk list concedes the likeliest outcome is 'shipping nothing.' Under this lens that risk is decisive: stars require a shipped artifact, and this is the largest v1 by a wide margin for a solo maintainer. Second: the launch narrative walks straight into 'why not FreshRSS?' — competing on incumbents' strongest ground with a v1 feature set, while the distinctive feature (vault export) has an awkward remote-mode story (notes land on the server's filesystem) until the unbuilt git-push destination ships. 'Web UI costs near-zero' underprices auth/session/responsive work. Contributor onboarding is worst of the three: auth surface, compat matrix, lifecycle supervision — a bigger system to understand before a first PR.

rationale: Under an OSS-adoption lens the question is expected stars × probability of shipping × contributor funnel. Server-first has the highest star ceiling (homelab distribution is the proven engine) but the lowest ship probability and the worst first-PR experience, and it forfeits the distinctive demo by making the reader look like another self-hosted web app while inviting the FreshRSS comparison it cannot win at v1. Desktop-first ships almost surely and has the cleanest install story, but it offers exactly one contribution surface (a Tauri app), zero scripting surface, and a hard cap from the sync question. core-plus-heads dominates: it keeps ~95% of desktop-first's low scope and its entire demo (the promote-to-vault GIF), adds a second package ecosystem (cargo install / brew / nixpkgs for the CLI) and a second demo medium (terminal asciinema — the format that lands with the Rust/HN audience), gives contributors a webview-free on-ramp into the risky code that CI enforces mechanically, and converts both losing designs' strengths into cheap options rather than v1 bets (server head → homelab/GReader mobile story later; client mode → FreshRSS cockpit later). It is also the design Alex's own raw/curio-rss.md pre-endorsed ('extract the Rust fetch/normalize core as a shared crate'), and the KP seam is strongest here: vault export + event log live in core, so CLI, desktop, and any future head export identically and the contracts are headlessly testable — the KP consumes files/schemas regardless of which head produced them.
grafts: From desktop-first: lead the README with the 'files are the API' trust frame and ship its one-command full-portability archive (OPML + events JSONL + settings + read-later) as `curio export --all` — the anti-lock-in pitch and the second-machine seeding story in one command.

From desktop-first: the vault manifest must live IN the vault (.curio/manifest.json), not only as a SQLite table, specifically for its wipe-and-reinstall reconciliation property — a fresh Curio install pointed at an existing vault reconciles instead of re-duplicating; document the two-machines-one-vault conflict recovery path it flagged.

From desktop-first: the explicit ipc_policy path gate — export/import paths accepted only from dialog-plugin picks or registered vault roots, never raw IPC strings — as a named module in the desktop head, plus its docs/migration-from-X guides (Omnivore/Pocket/NetNewsWire refugees are the adoption funnel).

From desktop-first: human-readable docs/schemas/frontmatter-v1.md and events-v1.md alongside the schemars JSON Schema artifacts, GOVERNANCE.md ('personal-first, community-welcome'), PRIVACY.md enumerating every network call class, DCO-not-CLA, and the theme-preload no-FOUC script + youtube-nocookie click-to-load facades as v1 layout polish.
From desktop-first: criterion cold-start budget in CI against the 50k fixture from the first alpha, and single-instance handling in the Tauri shell.

From server-first: the named-destination registry pattern — promotion API is always 'promote article X to destination NAME', never a filesystem path over the boundary — apply it to desktop IPC and CLI flags alike; it structurally kills the arbitrary-file-write class even if a sanitizer misses.

From server-first: refuse to open the SQLite DB on known network filesystems without an explicit override flag, with loud docs — preempts the inevitable 'Syncthing corrupted my database' issue-tracker plague that desktop-only readers all accumulate.

From server-first: a webhook sink option for the JSONL event log (file sink stays default) — near-free in curio-core's events module and makes the signals contract push-capable for automation users and the KP librarian.
From server-first: publish the perf harness as a public benchmark page (fixed runner hardware, 50k-article fixture, vs-Electron-incumbents framing) — turns the CI gate into marketing material.

From server-first: adopt its honest tiered 'what v1 does NOT do' sync section verbatim in the README — plainly stating no multi-device state sync, what the vault/git path covers, and which seam (server head or client mode) answers it later; preempting the top HN question in the README is worth real stars.

scores.design: Curio Desktop-First Monolith — "one app, files are the API"
scores.notes: Smallest total surface: zero server ops, no auth, no compat protocols, one state machine, one release pipeline. Two-year rot vectors are shared with every candidate (Tauri major churn, tauri-specta coupling, WebKitGTK distro variance, signing ceremony) plus two of its own: (a) the seven-crate purity boundary is policed only by convention — no shipped headless consumer proves it, so logic quietly accretes into src-tauri over two years of agent-driven patches; (b) KP automation requires the GUI running on the Mac — no cron/agent surface, which fights how Alex actually maintains things (agents, headless guests) and forecloses raw/curio-rss.md's own second candidate resolution (extract the core as a shared crate for the KP ingestion plane). CI cost hotspot: per-PR 3-OS `tauri build` plus a manual per-OS smoke matrix per release that a spare-time maintainer will skip by month six. Unofficial-API blast radius: excellent — layouts-only v1, enrichment feature-flagged, yt-dlp never bundled. WEAKEST CLAIM: "a headless CLI/MCP binary is a cheap post-v1 addition without designing it in now" — deferred-cheap claims are how boundaries rot; second weakest is "journal-merge is additive, no schema migration" while hand-waving clock-skew LWW, deleted-feed refs, and log compaction into a published-schema feature.

Best confidence-per-CI-minute of the three: all risky code (fetch/parse/store/export/events) tests headless under plain `cargo test` on one cheap Linux runner; the 3-OS Tauri matrix is a separate workflow that can run on merge/nightly only. The CLI head is the load-bearing maintenance idea: a mechanical tripwire (cargo-deny bans tauri in core's dep tree; the headless build breaks on any leak) instead of review-policed purity — exactly Alex's prefer-deterministic-tooling doctrine — AND the agent/cron surface (`curio fetch`, `curio export --vault`, `curio events tail`) that matches maintenance-by-agents and gives the KP a headless hook on a guest with zero Curio code. It is literally raw/curio-rss.md's candidate resolution ("extract the Rust fetch/normalize core as a shared crate") made structural, and the checksum-keyed frontmatter mirrors RFC-0018 lines 96/108 exactly. Unofficial-API blast radius equals design 1 (trait ports, feature flags, degrade-to-RSS). Costs over design 1: a second binary to release (trivial — no signing ceremony), schema-publishing machinery, and the in-tree curio-server stub. WEAKEST CLAIMS: "curio-cli doubles as headless E2E harness" — it never exercises the desktop state layer users actually touch, so the desktop head can rot behind green core CI (the tauri-driver smoke + manual matrix remain load-bearing); and the unbuilt curio-server crate in the workspace, which must be kept compiling through every refactor and dependency bump while shipping nothing, and invites contributor demand governance must then refuse.

Worst two-year rot profile for one person, by construction. (1) Auth turns every bug into a potential remote CVE with an urgent-response obligation a spare-time maintainer cannot honor — and the trust-positioned audience punishes the first one hardest (its own risk list says so). (2) GReader/Fever compat is unbounded ongoing manual QA against third-party client releases Alex cannot fix — Reeder/Capy update, quirks break, issues arrive; this is the highest-entropy surface in the whole comparison and it renews forever. (3) Release matrix doubles: docker+static binary+SBOM+minisign+systemd AND signed/notarized desktop installers. (4) A bug class no other design has: embedded-server lifecycle (WAL locks after force-kill, sleep/wake, AV on Windows loopback). Genuine strengths: real Playwright E2E against the shipped binary, sanitize-once-server-side, and the named-destination export registry are the best individual mechanisms in any candidate. WEAKEST CLAIM: "sync by construction, not bolted on... no second protocol" — it ships THREE protocols (native /v1 + GReader + Fever), each a compat commitment the moment one client attaches; the fleet-of-clients versioning problem it claims to avoid re-enters through the compat door. Runner-up: "the web UI costs near-zero since the frontend is already a web app" ignores auth flows, sessions, responsive layout, and a browser support matrix.

rationale: Under a one-person-plus-agents, two-year lens the question is: where does confidence come from when nobody is watching, and what breaks when the world moves? Designs 1 and 2 are near-twins on platform-matrix pain (same 3-OS Tauri desktop, same WebKitGTK exposure, same signing ceremony) and on unofficial-API blast radius (both defer Reddit/yt-dlp behind trait ports and feature flags, both refuse to bundle binaries — breakage is a provider outage, not a product outage). Design 3 is disqualified by lens: it converts a hobby project into an operated service category (remote auth surface, three public protocols, third-party mobile-client compat QA, doubled release targets, embedded-server lifecycle bugs) — each individually manageable, jointly the classic solo-OSS death spiral its own risk list names (\"the single likeliest failure mode is shipping nothing\"). The tiebreak between 1 and 2 is mechanical enforcement versus convention: design 2's shipped CLI head makes the core/shell boundary a CI-verified fact (cargo-deny ban + headless build) rather than a review discipline, which is the difference between a boundary that survives two years of agent-authored patches and one that erodes. It also has the best CI economics (risky code tests on one cheap headless runner; the expensive Tauri matrix can be merge/nightly-gated) and — decisive for this specific user — the CLI is the agent/cron surface that lets the Knowledge Plane consume Curio headlessly on a guest, matching raw/curio-rss.md's own \"extract the core as a shared crate\" resolution and RFC-0018's checksum-keyed ingestion contract without any KP-specific code. Design 2's liabilities are real but excisable: delete the unbuilt curio-server stub from the tree (keep it as a design doc), and treat \"CLI = E2E\" skepticism by keeping the tauri-driver smoke tier non-negotiable. Score gap to design 1 is deliberately small — if the CLI head's scope creeps beyond a thin clap wrapper over core, design 1's leaner instinct was right."
grafts: From server-first: the named export-destination registry — promotion can only reference a registered destination name, never a raw path over IPC. Replaces/augments core-plus-heads' dialog-picked paths in the desktop head; structurally kills the XSS-to-arbitrary-file-write primitive and shrinks the path-sanitization audit surface to one module.

From server-first: the network-filesystem tripwire — curio-store detects and refuses (with an explicit override flag) to open a DB on Syncthing/iCloud/NFS-class mounts, with loud docs that SQLite over file-sync silently corrupts. Converts the most predictable future support thread into a self-surfacing error, which is pure maintenance-load reduction.
From server-first: publish the criterion + 50k-fixture benchmark results as a public page per release — falsifiable perf claims are marketing that cannot rot into the PRD's unfalsifiable metrics.

From desktop-monolith: the designed v1.x file-based journal-merge path (per-device append-only ULID event journals in a user-synced folder; set-union of event ids + LWW per (article, state-dimension)) adopted as THE named post-v1 multi-device direction — strictly lower-ops for a solo maintainer than ever building curio-server. Pair with deleting the in-tree server stub: demote it to a docs/ design note so nothing unshipped has to keep compiling.

From desktop-monolith: human-readable docs/schemas/frontmatter-v1.md and events-v1.md with an explicit written breaking-change policy, published alongside the generated JSON Schema artifacts — the KP is consumer #1 of these contracts and prose contracts survive tooling churn that schemars output does not.

From desktop-monolith: the in-vault .curio/manifest.json as the reconciliation source of truth, so a wiped-and-reinstalled Curio re-adopts an existing vault instead of re-duplicating it, plus documented single-writer guidance and a manifest-conflict recovery path for two-machines-one-synced-vault users (pre-empts the project's first angry multi-device issue).

From desktop-monolith: the explicit config/data/cache platform-path split with a content-hash, LRU-evicted, size-capped image cache in the OS cache dir — XDG/backup/roaming hygiene core-plus-heads only partially specifies.

From desktop-monolith: pre-first-artifact blockers treated as architecture — updater private-key custody/rotation plan, 'Curio' trademark clearance, and the reverse-DNS identifier fix before any signed release; plus GOVERNANCE.md's 'personal-first, community-welcome' statement with a rehearsed anyone-can-cut-a-release runbook (bus-factor insurance that costs a page of markdown).

From desktop-monolith (CI discipline, applied strictly to the winner's workflow split): per-PR CI = headless core tests + corpus + bindings-diff on one Linux runner only; the 3-OS `tauri build` + tauri-driver smoke runs on merge-to-main/nightly/release — keeps two years of PR iteration cheap without giving up the platform gate.

scores.design: Curio Desktop-First Monolith — "one app, files are the API"
scores.notes: PRODUCT V1: strongest raw reading feel on paper — no HTTP hop, event-sourced undo, sanitize-at-ingest + readability + htmd gives full-text extraction now; the files-are-the-API trust story is genuinely marketable post-Omnivore. KP SEAM: excellent — manifest-in-vault (.curio/manifest.json) enabling wipe-and-reinstall reconciliation is the single best seam idea across all three candidates; versioned docs/schemas contracts match RFC-0018's checksum/dedupe ingestion spec exactly. MULTI-DEVICE: the disqualifier under this lens. The answer is 'vault + portability archive + future file-journal merge' — the design itself concedes it is 'a bet against the table-stakes list.' WEAKEST CLAIM: that per-device append-only journals in a Syncthing/iCloud folder merging by set-union + timestamp-LWW is 'an additive feature, no schema migration.' That is the NetNewsWire-iCloud failure shape (clock skew, partial file sync, no conflict UX), and even if it works it never produces a phone reading experience — Obsidian Mobile reading of *promoted* notes is not mobile RSS. Secondary weak claim: 'the market seat is literally empty' ignores that the seat is empty partly because desktop-only readers churn users to sync-backed ones. Retrofitting GReader client mode later inverts the data flow (local DB becomes a cache of remote truth) — closer to a state-layer rewrite than the risk list admits.

PRODUCT V1: reading experience is essentially identical to the monolith (same Tauri/Svelte-5 head, same sanitize-at-ingest/readability/htmd extraction, backend-owned counts, virtualized lists) — nothing about the core boundary costs the user anything at v1. MULTI-DEVICE: most credible no-rewrite path of the three because it is mechanically enforced, not promised: curio-cli ships in v1 and breaks CI the day the core stops being headless, so the seam a future curio-server (GReader/Fever → existing Capy Reader/Reeder Classic mobile clients) or StateBackend client mode needs is continuously proven. Two independent futures, neither committed. KP SEAM: best of the three for Alex specifically — export + events live in core, `curio export`/`curio events tail` gives the KP ingestion plane a scriptable headless surface on G11 from day one, and curio-types publishes semver'd JSON Schema artifacts (the external-consumer contract as a release artifact, not a doc). This is literally both candidate resolutions from raw/curio-rss.md at once (promotion-to-vault-via-git AND extractable Rust core). WEAKEST CLAIM: 'both futures are new heads, not rewrites.' The server head's auth/deployment/compat surface is undesigned (GReader compat is a client-quirk swamp), and client mode inverts local-DB-as-truth into local-DB-as-cache — event sourcing helps but the desktop query/state layer still gets rebuilt around remote cursors. Secondary risk unpriced: thin-head discipline can starve the desktop head of the UX polish users actually judge the product on.

MULTI-DEVICE: the only design that delivers sync + mobile AT v1 rather than eventually — GReader/Fever at launch means real phone apps day one, web UI covers couch reach, and 'multi-device is a base URL' is true by construction. For Alex's KP the deployment story is superb: curiod on the G11 always-on tier, git-push vault destination into the mirrored inbox/, /v1/events cursor for the librarian. PRODUCT V1: this is where it loses under the lens. The lens says sync/mobile 'eventually' but reading experience 'now' — and this design pays its v1 quality budget on auth, two release targets, compat matrices, and embedded-server lifecycle (WAL locks after force-kill, sleep/wake, AV-on-loopback) instead of on the reader. The <50ms cockpit feel depends on optimistic updates + SSE invalidation done excellently — exactly the polish a solo maintainer cuts, per its own risk list. Highest ship-risk of the three; 'joining the OSS-desktop graveyard by shipping nothing' is its own stated likeliest failure mode. WEAKEST CLAIM: 'sync-by-construction, no reconciliation layer invented later' — the remote-mode offline story IS a bolted-on mini-reconciliation layer (read cache + replay outbox + LWW that admittedly loses flips), and 'µs over UDS' misattributes where desktop latency actually comes from (query-cache invalidation, serialization, render), so the native-feel claim is the least falsified in the set. Also remote-mode vault export writing on the server's filesystem is an awkward seam until git-push destinations ship — the generic OSS user's intuition ('notes appear where I read') breaks.

rationale: Under the product/user lens the question decomposes as: (1) v1 reading experience with real full-text extraction, (2) credible no-rewrite path to sync/mobile which is demanded eventually, not now, (3) KP/vault seam quality. core-plus-heads dominates because it is the only candidate that wins or ties on all three axes. On (1) it ties the monolith — the user-facing v1 is the same Tauri/Svelte app with the same sanitize-at-ingest + readability + htmd extraction pipeline — while server-first demonstrably risks the v1 feel (HTTP-hop cockpit, embedded-server lifecycle bugs, solo-maintainer scope gravity) to buy a capability the lens only requires eventually. On (2) it beats the monolith decisively: the monolith's file-journal-merge story is the historically failing shape and never yields a phone, whereas core-plus-heads keeps BOTH credible futures open (server head speaking GReader/Fever to the existing healthy mobile-client ecosystem, or client mode into FreshRSS/Miniflux) and — critically — enforces the enabling boundary mechanically via the v1 CLI head breaking CI on any Tauri leakage, so the path is proven daily rather than promised. It loses (2) to server-first on delivery date but not on credibility, and the lens prices that correctly. On (3) it is the best fit for RFC-0018: export + events as core modules with published JSON Schema artifacts, plus a headless `curio export`/`curio events tail` surface the KP ingestion plane on G11 can drive with zero Curio code — it instantiates both candidate resolutions Alex already wrote in raw/curio-rss.md (promotion-to-vault-via-git as sync backbone AND the extractable fetch/normalize core), while staying fully generic for OSS. Its weakest claim ('new heads, not rewrites') is real but bounded: even if the server head costs more than advertised, the failure mode is delayed sync, not a compromised v1 reader — the reverse of server-first, whose failure mode is a mediocre v1 reader, which under this lens is fatal.
grafts: From monolith: the per-vault manifest's wipe-and-reinstall reconciliation semantics made explicit — a fresh Curio install pointed at an existing vault must reconcile against .curio/manifest.json instead of re-duplicating notes, plus a documented manifest-conflict recovery path for the two-machines-one-synced-vault case (the project's first angry issue otherwise).

From monolith: the one-command full-portability archive (OPML + article-state-as-events JSONL + settings + read-later) as a curio-cli subcommand — it is both the second-machine seeding story pre-sync and the anti-lock-in marketing artifact; core-plus-heads' CLI currently lacks it.

From monolith: human-readable versioned contract docs (docs/schemas/frontmatter-v1.md, events-v1.md) with a stated breaking-change policy alongside the machine JSON Schema artifacts — external consumers (the KP is consumer #1) integrate against prose + examples, not schemars output.

From monolith: the ipc_policy path gate — export/import paths accepted only from dialog-plugin picks or registered vault roots, never raw IPC strings — as an explicit desktop-head module, closing the XSS→arbitrary-file-write class even if a sanitizer regresses.

From monolith: event-log sink ergonomics — configurable sink directory (pointable at a synced folder or the vault's .curio/events/), daily rotation, cursor checkpointing — as the default JSONL projection behavior, not just `events tail`.

From server-first: design the events module's cursor semantics NOW as `events since <cursor>` so the JSONL export, the CLI tail, and the future server head's GET /v1/events?since= incremental-sync endpoint are the same cursor — this is the cheapest possible down-payment on the server head.

From server-first: named export destinations as the only promotion surface (promote article X to destination Y from a registered destination table) rather than paths-over-the-wire — a security and UX win that also pre-shapes the API the server head will expose.

From server-first: a git-push vault destination kind as a designed (v1.x) destination in curio-core's export module — for Alex it makes any Curio deployment push promoted notes into the KP's git-mirrored inbox/; for OSS it is the 'your notes follow you' feature no directory-only exporter has.

From server-first: Pocket/Omnivore/Readwise import in curio-cli — post-shutdown refugee onboarding is the highest-leverage adoption magnet for exactly this audience and costs little in a CLI that already parses OPML.

From server-first: the network-filesystem guard — refuse to open the SQLite DB on known network/synced filesystems without an explicit override flag, with loud docs — preempting the silent-corruption bug class that would otherwise be blamed on the app.

From server-first: publish the perf harness results as a public benchmark page (50k-article fixture, criterion numbers on fixed runners) — turns the performance differentiator into a falsifiable marketing asset instead of a PRD claim.

From server-first: webhook as an additional event-log sink kind alongside file sinks — trivial in core, and it lets downstream consumers (including a future KP librarian trigger) go push-based without polling files.
